persona.name: Haruka Fujimoto

occupation.title: DX Promotion Lead
occupation.description: Leads a cross-functional team of 5 tasked with identifying, piloting, and scaling digital tools across Lion's marketing and sales divisions. Acts as an internal evangelist for productivity tools and AI adoption. Bridges the gap between IT governance and business units who want to move faster. Reports to the CDO and presents monthly to the executive committee on DX initiative progress.

persona.education: M.B.A. from Hitotsubashi University (ICS program, taught in English). B.A. in Sociology from Sophia University. Spent two years at Accenture Japan before joining Lion, which gave her consulting frameworks she still uses daily.
long_term_goals: Become the first female CDO at a major Japanese consumer goods company
Build a DX playbook that other traditional manufacturers can replicate
Publish thought leadership on AI adoption in Japanese enterprises
Raise her daughter to be bilingual and globally minded
persona.style: Polished and articulate, with a consulting-trained ability to structure any argument into three points. Dresses in smart contemporary business style — blazers with a creative edge, statement earrings. Switches fluidly between formal Japanese in executive meetings and casual English-peppered conversation with her team. Creates beautiful PowerPoint decks that simplify complex concepts. In meetings she's the facilitator type — draws out quiet voices and synthesizes group input into action items. Active on LinkedIn and sometimes posts about DX in Japanese manufacturing.

traits: Strategic thinker who connects individual tools to organizational transformation
Persuasive communicator who adapts her message to the audience
Genuinely excited about AI and its potential for Japanese enterprises
Diplomatic navigator of internal politics — knows when to push and when to wait
Data-driven but understands that change management is emotional
Sometimes overestimates how ready the organization is for new tools
Balances ambition with a pragmatic understanding of Japanese corporate pace

interests: Enterprise AI adoption and digital transformation strategy
Meeting productivity tools — actively evaluates 2-3 per quarter
Japanese contemporary art — visits galleries on weekends
Cooking Japanese home meals with a modern twist
Following DX case studies from other Japanese manufacturers
likes: AI tools with clear ROI metrics she can present to executives
Products that offer enterprise trials with dedicated onboarding
Vendors who understand Japanese corporate decision-making culture
Tools that generate structured outputs (action items, summaries) automatically
When a pilot program succeeds and skeptics become advocates
Collaborative workshops where teams discover tools together
Saturday afternoons at a gallery with her daughter
dislikes: Tools that work great in demos but fail in real enterprise environments
Vendors who don't have Japanese-language support or local presence
The slow ringi (approval circulation) process for tool procurement
When business units adopt shadow IT and then ask for support
Being dismissed as 'just the DX person' when she raises strategic points
AI tools that can't handle Japanese language meetings accurately
Missing her daughter's bedtime because of late executive meetings

beliefs: AI meeting tools are the easiest DX win because everyone hates taking meeting minutes
Successful tool adoption requires executive sponsorship AND grassroots enthusiasm
Japanese companies won't adopt AI tools unless they work flawlessly in Japanese
DX is not about technology — it's about changing how people work together
skills: Enterprise tool evaluation with ROI modeling

general: Runs structured pilot programs for new tools: 2-week trial → survey → decision
Maintains a comparison spreadsheet of AI meeting tools she's evaluated
Presents tool adoption metrics monthly to the CDO and executive committee
Networks actively with DX leads at other companies through industry events and LinkedIn

morning: Wakes at 6:15, gets her daughter ready for nursery
Train to Ryogoku office, reviews meeting prep on the way
At desk by 8:45 with coffee from the office machine
workday: Morning: DX team standup, then stakeholder alignment meetings
Lunch: often working lunch with a vendor demo or networking
Afternoon: pilot program reviews, executive prep, cross-functional workshops
Leaves by 18:30 to pick up daughter — non-negotiable boundary
evening: Picks up daughter by 19:00, dinner and bath
Reads to her daughter until bedtime at 20:30
Catches up on Slack and email until 22:00
Reads a DX or business book for 20 minutes before bed
weekend: Saturday morning: takes daughter to a park or children's museum
Afternoon: gallery visit or coffee with industry contacts
Sunday: family time, cooking a proper meal, household tasks
Sunday evening: reviews the week ahead and prepares executive slide updates

health.physical: Healthy but time-starved for exercise. Used to run but stopped after her daughter was born. Does occasional yoga at home from YouTube. Annual health check is clean — tries to eat balanced meals despite the time pressure.
health.mental: Manages the cognitive load of being a working mother, DX evangelist, and corporate politician simultaneously. Occasionally feels imposter syndrome in all-male executive meetings. Her daughter's joy is her reset button. Considers herself resilient but admits she's running at capacity.

relationships.name: Koichi Fujimoto
relationships.description: 38, senior engineer at a robotics company. Supportive of Haruka's career ambitions. They share household duties roughly equally, which is unusual in their social circle and something they're both proud of.

relationships.name: Mio Fujimoto
relationships.description: 4, attends a bilingual nursery in Sumida-ku. Haruka's primary motivation for maintaining work boundaries. Starting to form her own personality — curious and talkative.

relationships.name: Yusuke Okada
relationships.relation: Former Accenture colleague and DX peer
relationships.description: 37, DX lead at a food manufacturer. They meet monthly to exchange notes on tool evaluations and organizational change tactics.

relationships.name: Kyoko Takeuchi
relationships.description: 52, former McKinsey partner who joined Lion to lead digital transformation. Haruka's executive sponsor and career mentor. Demanding but fair.
